texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work presents the results of the techno-economic analysis of a proposed 100 m 3 day -1 desalination plant based on existing plate and frame MD technology coupled with a static solar collector field as main heat supply. The experimental results of one commercial and two pre-commercial MD modules characterized previously under real operational conditions were used. Three different cost-effective solar technologies (evacuated tube collectors (ETCs), flat plate collectors (FPCs) and compound parabolic collectors (CPCs)) were considered for the analysis and the results were compared to a fossil source. For the dimensioning of the solar field, meteorological data from Almeria (South-Spain) where the experimental campaign took place were selected. A thorough economical assessment was performed to calculate the cost of water (WPC) for the different considered scenarios. The best results obtained show a WPC in the range of 10 -11.30 € m -3 for the combination of the most efficient MD module and solar collector. The fossil-driven option showed a similar (i.e. 7.19 € m -3 ) yet lower WPC. Although the MD technology here analyzed is in an early stage of development, the results obtained suggest solar MD can be already competitive with PV-RO and PV-ER according to reported data for similar capacities (~100 m 3 day -1 ).

texts.orig: reasons. However, it is an energy-intensive process and those technologies that can be coupled to a renewable energy source are preferred. MDis a thermal separating process that uses a hydrophobic membrane to create a liquid vapor interface from which water is evaporated. It is considered suitable to be coupled with solar thermal energy because of its low demanding operational requirements (i.e. low temperatures and pressures) and the features of the membranes used in the process (i.e. relatively big pore size, low chemical interaction, thermal resistance and highly hydrophobic nature) which make them less prone to fouling and a good candidate to stand intermittent and stand-alone operation [1,2]. MD has the additional advantage of a small footprint (similar to that of Reverse Osmosis (RO)) and the need of low grade heat (waste heat, low-temperature solar thermal power, geothermal power, etc.).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, real operational data of three different pilot-plant sized plate and frame MD modules (one air gap and two liquid gap MD modules) coupled to a solar field were used to design a medium size (100 m 3 day -1 ) solar MD desalination plant. Three different solar collectors' technologies suitable for the MD temperature range: flat plate collectors (FPCs), compound parabolic collectors (CPCs) and evacuated tube collectors (ETCs) were used for the simulation over a year of operation. Based on the results a thorough techno-economic assessment of each of the case study proposed was performed. A fossil energy driven scenario was added to the analyses for comparison purposes.

texts.text: 2. MD systems and their energy use

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As pointed out before, few reliable WPC data of MD systems can be found in literature. A thorough compilation of the dispersion in energy consumption and water production costs can be found in Khayet's review on solar MD desalination economics [7] and in Saffarini's technical evaluation on MD desalination solar systems [6]. Particularly, the figures given for the STEC on which some authors base their calculations seem to be hardly realistic. For example, exceptionally low figures such as 1.25 kWhth m -3 [9] and WPC of $4.04 m -3 [10] supported by very little technical data have been published and referenced [11]. Khayet's review includes references to Memstill® technology, claimed to be able to operate with a STEC as low as 22 -66 kWhth m -3 and a corresponding WPC of $0.26 -0.54 m -3 [12] the latter being in the range or even below the WPC of commercial large-scale RO systems. Similar examples gathered in the review include a geothermal AGMD system by Bouguecha et al. [13] in which three MD modules were arranged in series reducing the total STEC to 30.83 kWhthm -3 . However, no information was provided regarding flow rates, operating temperatures or SPFs. A similar example is the AGMD system described by Gazagnes et al. [14] who reported a STEC of 1 kWhthm -3 (very close to the minimum isothermal reversible work of separation which is in the range of 1.09 to 0.7 kWh m -3 depending on the recovery ratio [15]) without providing any detailed discussion.

texts.orig: In general, the reported values are based on theoretical calculations or experimental results from lab scale layouts and membrane areas of a few cm 2 . They are normally operated under strictly controlled and optimal conditions (e.g. very high linear velocities, high trans-membrane temperature difference or Δ T, and optimum flow distribution) and almost neither mass nor temperature limited, resulting in high fluxes and relatively low thermal consumptions. The authors perform analyses of local mass and heat transfer on these systems and project those results to the large scale. This highlights an urgent need for real analysis of MD pilot plants based on energy consumption [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The possibility of heat recovery in large scale MD modules is critical for the industrial implementation of the technology. However, most of the thermal analyses performed in literature focus only on the heat used in the distillation process itself while the heat transferred to the condenser stream or the heat wasted through the brine blowdown and their potential reuse has not been given much attention. In large scale modules, where the membrane areas are in the order of a few m 2 , flow distribution conditions are poorer and heat conduction losses are significant when compared to a membrane coupon in a lab-scale layout. As a consequence the trans-membrane Δ T is significantly reduced along the membrane area, hence the distillate production is also reduced and the final heat consumption increases (i.e. low efficiency and GOR values). In order to recover part of the heat, commercial modules are designed to use the heat transferred to the cold stream as a preheated feed inlet. This way, the blowdown brine temperature will be lower and so will be the wasted heat. The heat input to the system is consequently determined by the temperature difference between the warmed-up condenser outlet and the feed inlet.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD is a membrane and a thermal process; therefore both thermal energy consumption and membrane flux are important to evaluate its performance. Some studies have already pointed out that there is a tradeoff between these two (STEC and SPF) [6,19,20]. As stated before, MD modules use the cold stream as preheated feed inlet. The cold stream heats up by means of the latent heat of condensation of the distillate produced but also by sensible heat conduction through the membrane. MD modules should be designed to maximize the first fraction (latent heat of condensation) although in experimental studies with real MD modules, the amount of heat transferred by conduction through the membrane/module surpasses notably that of the condensed vapor [19]. In the latter reference, the authors reported that the percentage the latent heat represented in the overall heat recovery was lower (10 -30%) than that of the sensible heat (40 -55%) in all the experiments performed with a concentration of 35 g L -1 . Generally, a MD module behaves like a heat exchanger with mass transfer, howeverwhenthemoduledesignis not the ideal one; the mass transfer is so little that it does not play an important role in the overall heat transfer. That is why heat recovery in MD is mostly achieved by contact time, which can be reached via increased residence times inside the MD module (e.g. low specific feed flow rates) or via increased contact surfaces (e.g. large channel lengths). Low flow rates reduce the electrical power consumption and enhance the overall energy efficiency. However, lower flow rates have the negative effect of increasing the temperature polarization which tends to reduce the permeate flux and the SPF. An increased channel length (and therefore larger membrane area) also contributes to a lower SPF. Higher feed flow rates increase SPF but also result in an increased STEC. Maximum thermal efficiency and maximum distillate output in MD are therefore conflicting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multi-staging is another possibility to increase the residence time/ channel length of the MD module. There is also a complementary effect in the case of the multi-stage strategy which is to maintain the operative transmembrane temperature difference in a greater portion of the membrane area enhancing the distillate production and increasing the latent heat recovery. This is possible because a multi-stage layout allows maintaining a higher feed flow rate in each of the stages, reducing the temperature polarization and maximizing the distillate production. In each of the stages, the specific feed flow rate is greater than the total specific feed flow rate of the whole layout, so the production of distillate is maximized at the same time the residence time/channel length is increased. This way, the production is less penalized and the thermal efficiency is increased [19]. A more detailed review of the different strategies that MD solar systems have used to increase the thermal performance can be found in [6]. A specific comparison between the compact plate and frame module and the multi-stage layout with same total membrane area, under real operation conditions can be found in [19].

texts.text: In this work, experimental data of three different pilot-size MD modules (from now on modules A, B and C) tested by the authors under real solar operational conditions at Plataforma Solar de Almería (PSA) (Spain) [19,21] were considered. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both the AGMD and the LGMD configurations have the advantage of employing seawater (simulated seawater in this case [19,21]) in the refrigeration stream, allowing reusing it as preheated feed. For that reason, the refrigeration stream is fed into the system in countercurrent flow to maximize the heat exchange. There are slight differences between the AGMD and the LGMD configurations. In LGMD the space between the membrane and the condensing surface is normally filled with the produced distillated water that normally exits from the top of the MD module, whereas in AGMD the permeate water leaves the module from the bottom. Some authors have reported slightly higher (2.2 -6.5%) permeate fluxes for LGMD when compared to AGMD. This enhancement has been attributed to the shorter path the vapor has to cross in LGMD and the lower mass transfer resistance of the liquid layer [25] compared to that of the air gap. Additionally and despite the fact the thermal conductivity of water is one order of magnitude greater than that of air, the overall thermal efficiency of the LGMD is also slightly better than that of the AGMD.

texts.orig: The total membrane area is the same in modules B and C (~9 m 2 ) while the single module A's membrane area is comparatively smaller (2.8 m 2 ). They are very differently designed modules too. Inside module Athefeed flow is divided into 10 channels, being the maximum channel length less than 0.5 m. Modules B and C have fewer but longer channels (~1.5 m) which provide a longer contact time. In module B the feed flow rate is divided into six channels while module C has only three channels. As a result, the feed flow velocities and the specific feed fluxes reached in the three modules are very different. Their respective characteristics and operational parameters have been summarized in Table 1.

texts.orig: To compensate for the poor heat recovery design of module A and the larger channel length/membrane area of modules B and C, a three stage strategy was adopted. This increased the total membrane area of module A to 8.4 m 2 , being more comparable to that of modules B and C. Amongst the tested modules, Module B allows the highest maximum feed flow rate (1560 L h -1 ). However, the specific feed flow rate per module (173.3 L h -1 m -2 ) when compared to that of modules A and C (428.6 and 380 L h -1 m -2 , respectively) is much lesser. On the other hand, when the overall specific feed flow rate per layout is compared (173.3, 142.9 and 126.6 L h -1 m -2 for modules B, A and C respectively) the result is the opposite. All the modules were tested under similar conditions at the demonstration MD plant built for this purpose in PSA, Spain. The MD plant, described somewhere else [21], was directly coupled to an existing field of CPC solar collectors. The experiments were run during solar hours throughout different periods of time no shorter than 3 months.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the dimensioning of the MD plant, experimental data of the performanceof the MD modules under real operation conditions (MD plant coupled to a solar field) have been used. In the particular case of the MD modules here selected and because of their early stage of development, the observed penalty in the distillate production when employing lower feed flow rates is only slightly counterbalanced by a small reduction of the STEC [19,21]. Therefore, the chosen criterion for this study was to maximize the distillate production and so the optimal operational conditions for each of the modules were selected accordingly. These are maximum feed flow rates for each of the modules (see Table 1) and feed and condenser temperatures of 80 °C and 30 °C, respectively. The most relevant performance parameters (i.e. recovery ratio (RR), SPF, GOR and temperature of the condenser outlet/distillate) of each of the modules when operating under the chosen conditions determined experimentally in previous works [19,21] have been summarized in Table 2.

texts.orig: 3.2. Solar collectors
texts.text: 3.2. Solar collectors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The selection of the solar collector technology is based on the temperature range of operation. Tracking collectors are suitable for applications that require high operation temperatures ( N 200 °C) while nontracking collectors reach lower temperatures. Normally, non-tracking collectors are non-concentrating ones and vice versa, although there are exceptions (i.e. compound parabolic collectors (CPCs)). MD maximum operation temperatures are around 80 -90 °C, therefore it is possible to use any of the static (non-tracking) solar collector technologies. To date, the most cost-effective amongst non-tracking collectors are: flat plate collectors (FPCs), evacuated tube collectors (ETCs), and compound parabolic collectors (CPCs).

Their efficiency is usually represented for a given radiation value (i.e. 1000 W m -2 ) as a function of the difference between the ambient temperature ( Tamb ) and the average between the inlet and outlet temperatures of the heat transfer fluid inside the collector ( Tcol ) (Fig. 2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be seen in Fig. 2, the FPC collector shows a slightly better performance at low values of Tcol -Tamb , but as Tcol -Tamb increases its efficiency decreases rapidly. CPC collector shows a similar behavior, however its overall efficiency is lower than that of the FPC. In general, ETC technology is less sensitive to solar irradiance variations than FPC or CPC, being the associated efficiency decrease less pronounced. For the collectors here considered, the efficiency of the ETC far exceeds the other two types, throughout the whole temperature range and even at lower values of irradiance. However, ETCs are much more expensive than FPC and CPC, and their durability is much lower (maximum period for the vacuum to be kept inside the tubes of an ETC is around 7 -10 years, after which the tubes must be replaced) which makes their maintenance costs much higher.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.1. Solar field dimensioning
texts.text: 3.2.1. Solar field dimensioning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A north -south orientation and a tilt angle equal to the local latitude have been considered for the designing of the static solar field in all the studied scenarios. Solar fields are dimensioned for a specific design point (specific geographical location and specific date, including time). The selection of the design point determines the year-round thermal output of the solar field. If the design point was chosen in summer, the resulting solar field size would be too small to deliver the required thermal load during winter. On the contrary, if the design point was chosen in winter, the resulting larger solar field would deliver more thermal power than needed in the summer. Therefore, the design point has to be chosen in order to avoid large seasonal differences. Taking these considerations into account, the design point chosen was the 16th of October at solar noon, which corresponds to sun zenith and presents greater stability of the direct solar irradiation. The location chosen was Almeria, Spain (Lat. 36.8° N, long. 2.4° E, alt. 21 m). The irradiation and ambient temperature data of the selected location have been taken from a typical meteorological year using the Meteonorm® software. Fig. 3 shows the monthly averaged global irradiation over tilted plane (36.8°) ( Gk ) and ambient temperature ( Tamb ) data generated for the chosen location.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once the solar field was dimensioned for each of the case studies proposed (i.e. combinations of each MD module and each solar collector type) the annual energy delivered was computed in order to calculate the resulting capacity factor (CF) defined as the ratio between the thermal energy delivered annually by the solar field and the total thermal energy required by the MD plant. In those cases where the CF of the solar field (as per the design point) resulted to be lower than the required CF the number of rows was increased by a factor (Solar Multiple or SM) in order to increase the power output. To make use of the thermal power delivered by the solar field the storage system hours have to be adjusted too. A simulation tool of the annual solar field performance that iterates both the SM and the storage hours in order to obtain a total CF of 25 and 50% respectively has been used. Both results (CF of 25 and 50%) have been analyzed in the economic study.

texts.orig: need solar collectors that can heat seawater directly, which is complicated from the operational point of view and are more expensive since they require salt-water resistant materials. Also, the fact that MD is a non-isothermal process and that conduction is the dominant heat transfer mechanism makes it necessary to maintain stable operation conditions to lessen the thermal inefficiencies [21]. A two loop layout is therefore more suitable for the operation of bigger systems like the 100 m 3 day -1 plant here proposed. In this kind of design, the desalination loop and the solar loop are connected through a heat exchanger. In order to achieve steadiness, a water-based thermal storage system has been included. The thermal storage allows coping with the variations of the solar irradiance along the day and extending the operation beyond sunshine hours. A second heat exchanger between the solar field andtheheat storage tank has been included to protect the solar field operation from freezing, corrosion, etc. The heated water is pumped from the water storage to supply thermal energy to the process. Finally, to guarantee a 24 h operation, a fossil fueled auxiliary system based on natural gas has been also considered. A simple scheme of the layout is represented in Fig. 4.

texts.orig: The thermal power load of the desalination process is defined by the GOR of each of the modules considered, the capacity of the plant (100 m 3 day -1 ) and the operation hours (24 h). The solar field is designed to supply the thermal power load required instantaneously as per the design point, however if there is an excess of thermal energy, the storage system can accumulate it depending on its capacity which is defined by the thermal power load and the hours of storage.

texts.orig: Thermal losses in the piping and thermal storage have been neglected however, a 5 °C drop has been assumed for each of the heat exchangers in the circuit. The model assumes a constant flow rate in the solar field. If the temperature and therefore the power delivered by the solar field and the heat already stored (if any) are not enough to supply the MD plant, the model calculates the power the gas boiler has to provide at a certain temperature (in this case the minimum useful temperature has been set to 85 °C accounting for the heat exchanger losses). A continuous flow boiler is considered here to maximize the power delivered by the solar field. On an hourly basis, the model calculates the power supplied by the solar field, the excess (if any) that can be stored and the power that has to be supplied by the auxiliary system (gas boiler). Overall, the model calculates the capacity factor (CF) of the solar field.

texts.orig: The costs related to the MD plant investment and maintenance have been obtained from desalination contractors and correspond to typical values of RO plants however they coincide with the very few values found in the MD literature [12,17,34]. The total electrical consumption of the MD plant has been calculated taking into account the following partial costs from [33]: intake and pretreatment (0.55 kWhe m -3 ), leakage (0.12 kWhe m -3 ), external pumping (0.32 kWhe m -3 ) and MD circuit (0.25 kWhe m -3 ). The membrane replacement in RO and ED systems depends largely on the raw water quality and is between 5% and 20%. Here we have assumed a value similar to that of Saffarini et al. [34] a 10%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Regarding the brine disposal, in RO systems considering outfall construction (dilution system) including capital, operation and maintenance costs can be around 0.03 -0.3 $ m -3 [35]. The brine produced during the MD desalination process as described in this work can be disposed directly into the sea. This is considered an environmentally friendly practice for two main reasons: the low RR of the desalination process (maximum RR achieved according to experimental data is below 5%) which corresponds to a maximum concentration of 105.3% of salts in the brine (i.e. 36.8 g L -1 in dissolved salts) and the absence of chemical pretreatment. As a result of these considerations this aspect of the MD plant can be considered as negligible and wasn't included in the analysis, as in any case, compared to the rest of the costs would mean a very small percentage of the total WPC. Regarding the MD module, membrane prices vary widely in literature, in this study a price of 500 $ (365 € ) per MD unit has been considered for all the modules since they all have almost the same membrane area (~9 m 2 ). If we consider Banat and Jwaied [32] criteria from which the membrane cost represents a 33.3% of the total membrane module cost, our study considers a price of $18.5 per m 2 of membrane area. Compared to the prices assumed in literature: Banat and Jwaied $36 m -2 [32], Al-Obaidani et al. $90 m -2 [37] or Drioli et al. $116 m -2 the cost assumed here is much less; however the plant capacity considered is also much bigger. If we compare it to the large system MD module cost reported by Banat and Jwaied [32] ($4320), or Bougcheca et al. [13] ($2100), the cost assumed in this work is significantly lower. However the cost proposed here is closer to that of commercial RO modules and for that reason it is considered as a good compromise.

texts.orig: the number of modules and membrane area needed for the same capacity is the largest. Module A's design is not optimized for heat recovery as its specific feed flow rate is relatively high and its total membrane area is small (Table 1). Although module A was arranged in a three stage layout to increase the membrane area and has relatively enhanced flow distribution, characteristic of a small module, its channel length is three times shorter than those of modules B and C. Module B has a higher specific feed flow rate than module A (Table 1), however its total feed flow rate and membrane area are larger resulting in a slightly better performance (higher distillate output). Module C combines the smallest specific flow rate, a multi-staging heat recovery strategy with a relatively large channel length, resulting in the best performance (i.e. lowest STEC and smallest Ni) amongst the three MD modules. As explained in the previous sections, lower specific flow rates mean increased times of residence and higher heat recovery ratios. However, it has been demonstrated experimentally [19,20] that although this significantly lowers the STEC of the module, has a much lesser influence on increasing the distillate output. This is directly related to the trade-off between heat recovery and distillate production already discussed in previous sections.

Regarding the MD modules, the number of rows was much less in the case of the combinations with the module C. As explained before the number of rows is related to the thermal power needed by the process and module C's thermal power consumption is five times less than its counterparts. As per the CF, in order to achieve a 25% (meaning that the remaining 75% thermal power has to be provided by the gas boiler) a SM of 2 was needed for all the FPC and CPC combinations studied. For all the ETC combinations, there was no need for increasing the number of collector rows (i.e. SM = 1) or using a thermal storage system (i.e. Storage (h) = 0) to get a CF of a 25%. In fact, the resulting CF was already ~27%. texts.orig: Generally speaking, the final cost of the produced water was the smallest when the gas boiler is used as only heat source. The lowest calculated WPC (i.e. 7.19 € m -3 ) corresponded to the combination of module C (the most efficient MD module) with the gas boiler (the lowest capital and maintenance cost option). The lowest solar WPC corresponding to the ETC-Module C combination for a CF of 25% (i.e. 9.96 € m -3 ) was a 40% larger than the lowest calculated WPC (i.e. fossil-only). The differences between the solar technologies employed are less significant and between the least efficient one (i.e. CPC) to the most efficient one (i.e. ETC) the general reduction of the costs was around 15%. The increment of the CF from a 25% to a 50% caused a WPC raise in the range of 12 to 38% depending on the solar collector (i.e. 38% in the case of the CPC, 30% in the case of the FPC and 12% in the case of the ETC). This result is the reflection of the collector' efficiency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When a CF of 25% was considered (Fig. 5 left), the differences between the chosen solar collectors were minimal. The solar field Capex and the additional fuel consumption costs were the main contributors to the WPC. They represented a 50% and a ~45% of the total WPC respectively, followed by the O&M costs (4 -5%) and the storage costs (~1%). The rest (i.e. MD Capex and electrical consumption) were practically insignificant ( b 0.5%). It is worth noticing that even though the CF has been fixed to a 25% meaning that the remaining 75% has to be provided by the fossil back-up, the Capex of the solar field exceeded or was close to 50% of the total WPC in all three cases (i.e. FPC, CPC and ETC). Solar thermal energy is still expensive when compared to fossil fuels (in terms of capital investment) however; the environmental benefits of the renewable desalination option are unarguable. When a 50% CF was considered (Fig. 5 right) the percentage that the solar field represented logically increases (60 -75%of the total WPC) but also the performance differences between the solar collectors became more relevant. The superior efficiency of the ETC collector increased this percentage only a ~13% while the FPC collector increased it a ~15% and the CPC collector a ~24%. The absolute cost distribution (Table 7) of the WPC for the two considered CFs (25 and 50%) and the different MD modules for the case of the CPC and the ETC collectors has been represented in Fig. 6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two main results can be easily inferred from these graphs. The first one; the less efficient the chosen solar collector the less cost-effective the increase of the CF will result (i.e. an average of 38% increase in the final WPC for the CPC collector combinations compared to an average of a 11% increase in the case of the ETC when the CF is doubled). And the second one; the efficiency of the MD modules in the use of the energy plays a crucial factor in the final cost of the water. The superior efficiency of module C (by a factor of 5 when compared to modules A and B) is able to reduce the total WPC by 80%. The exponential impact of the thermal efficiency of the MD modules on the final water cost can be seen in the following graph (Fig. 7) where the cost of water of the ETC with a 50% CF and the gas-boiler (fuel) options has been represented as a function of the GOR of the modules according to our cost calculations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The WPC is reduced exponentially when the efficiency (i.e. greater GOR) of the modules increases. According to our extrapolations, the WPC will be below 1 € m -3 only when the GOR reaches values near 30 (i.e. ~22 kWhthm -3 for a 100 m 3 day -1 plant and a distillate temperature of 65 °C) in the case of the fossil option and near a GOR of 40 (i.e. ~16 kWhthm -3 for a 100 m 3 day -1 plant, a distillate temperature of 65 °C and a CF of 50%) in the case of the most efficient solar driven option (ETC). Comparing the results of this work with other solar driven desalination MD technologies of a similar capacity range (1 -500 m 3 day -1 ) including: real systems, model results and theoretical exercises as reported in literature we found great data disparity (Figs. 8 and 9).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: project not only in terms of WPC but in GOR and specific flux too. The similarities in the type of data (real operation) and the layout (MD modules coupled with solar thermal energy) between the SMADES publications and ours explain the comparable results. The results published by Kullab et al. [22] projecting a cost of 1.85 € m -3 for a ~50 GOR system are also in good agreement with our extrapolations. However, these two graphs clearly represent the already highlighted in previous sections data disparity. The differences within the reported literature values are particularly important when predicting the WPC for higher GOR and specific flux systems. As discussed previously, very often the values reported in literature do not correspond to real operated systems. Instead, data obtained under laboratory-controlled conditions are simply extrapolated resulting in hardly reliable predictions. The graphs are also helpful to spot some incongruence. For example, the GOR value (~20) reported by Bougcheca et al. [13] for an experimental AGMD set-up operated in solar conditions corresponding to a STEC of 111 kj kg -1 and the specific flux (2.4 kg h -1 m -2 ) is hardly achievable. In fact, according to our calculations for the system (i.e. specific flux of 2.4 kg h -1 m -2 operating at a RR of 25% and a temperature of 343 K) we have estimated a GOR of ~1.4. However, important information to correctly estimate the GOR is missing in the article. In the case of Safarini et al. theoretical analysis for an AGMD system [34], a specific flux of 22 kg h -1 m -2 is assumed. We believe that such a high average specific flux value is difficult to achieve in a MD pilot-scale module. Also, the authors assumed a quite high feed flow rate (i.e. 1 kg s -1 ) which can explain the low GOR (~3) of the system despite its high specific flux. In any case and from the experimental data collected in literature, it seems hard to decrease the costs below 10 € m -3 . We should also keep in mind that the AGMD configuration results to be the most expensive one [34].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When comparing the results of this work to some other combinations of desalination technologies (Table 8) and renewables for systems in the range of the one proposed here, solar-MD can be competitive with PV-RO and PV-EDR but definitely not with other much more mature thermal technologies such as MED or MSF. However, as seen in this work, the variability of factors that plays a role in the final WPC of a solar-MD system makes it difficult to extrapolate data and find comparable results.
